title: Scrapped Ideas
subtitle: Archived concepts, alternate directions, and experiments I intentionally set aside while shaping this portfolio.

items.name: Dock Physics v1.txt
items.summary: An early dock animation pass that felt fun in isolation but too noisy once the rest of the interface calmed down.
content: The first dock prototype had oversized bounce reactions and too much magnet-like motion between neighboring icons.
It was visually impressive for a moment, but it kept pulling attention away from the actual portfolio content.
I archived it because the stronger choice was to make the dock feel polished and alive, not theatrical.

items.name: Alt Hero Direction.png
items.summary: A brighter landing treatment that looked polished, but diluted the macOS desktop illusion I wanted to preserve.

items.name: Portfolio Story v1
items.summary: An archived content structure that explained too much too early instead of letting the interface do the storytelling.
content: This version leaned heavily into guided storytelling before the user could explore the desktop for themselves.
It made the portfolio feel more like a slide deck than a system people could discover at their own pace.
I kept the notes because the content thinking was useful, but the interaction model was wrong for this product.

items.name: Reading Dashboard.png
items.summary: A content-heavy dashboard concept that was elegant, but too specific to one product direction for the main portfolio shell.

items.name: Why I Keep an Archive.txt
items.summary: A short note on why I keep failed directions around instead of deleting them completely.
content: I like keeping the directions that did not ship because they still explain the taste behind the final decisions.
An archive turns discarded work into evidence of iteration rather than invisible effort.
For a portfolio, that matters because process is often just as important as the finished screen.
